'''Bob Fingerman''' (born [[August 25]], [[1960s#1964|1964]]) is an American comic book creator, who wrote and pencilled the [[Star Wars comic books|comic]] "[[Fred Jawa (comic story)|Fred Jawa]]."

==Sources==
Best known for his comic series ''Minimum Wage'', Fingerman's contributions to the world of comic books have been many and varied. In 1984, while still a student at New York's School of Visual Arts, he produced work for Harvey Kurtzman (creator of [[Wikipedia:Mad magazine|''Mad magazine'']]) on the short-lived young readers anthology ''NUTS!'' At the same time Fingerman also signed a contract to produce a series of comical parodies of the Italian comic series, [[Wikipedia:RanXerox|''RanXerox'']], exclusively for the European market, which ran in such magazines as France's [[Wikipedia:L'Écho des savanes|''L'Écho des savanes'']] and ''Comics USA'', and Spain's infamous ''El Vibora''. Fingerman toiled in the disparate realms of children's satire, pornography, sci-fi and illustration, producing work regularly for [[Wikipedia:Cracked|''Cracked'']] magazine, Al Goldstein's infamous tabloid, ''Screw'', [[Wikipedia:Penthouse (magazine)|''Penthouse'']], ''Hot Talk'', [[Wikipedia:Heavy metal|''Heavy metal'']], [[Wikipedia:National Lampoon (magazine)|''National Lampoon'']], and [[Wikipedia:High Times|''High Times'']]. He also worked as an illustrator for the [[Wikipedia:Village Voice|''Village Voice'']], ''Video Review'', [[Wikipedia:Business Week|''Business Week'']], [[Wikipedia:Guitar World|''Guitar World'']], and many other periodicals.

In 1990 he decided to focus on doing comics. He did a yearlong stint on ''The [[Wikipedia:Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles|Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles]]'', as well as several titles for the Eros Comix line of adult comics. Among those were ''Skinheads in Love'' and ''Bloodsucker'', a collaboration with punk icon Lydia Lunch. He also created covers and short stories for [[Dark Horse Comics]], and DC Comics' [[Wikipedia:Vertigo Comics|Vertigo]] imprint. In 1993 Fingerman wrote and drew his first graphic novel, ''White Like She'', a sci-fi social satire about a middle-aged black man whose brain is transplanted into a white teenage girl's head. Upon completion of this purely fictional work, Fingerman decided to turn his attention inward and mine his own life for source material. The result was the semi-autobiographical series, [[Wikipedia:Minimum Wage|''Minimum Wage'']], which in 2003 was collected and extensively reworked as the Fantagraphics graphic novel, [[Wikipedia:Beg the Question|''Beg the Question'']] (and which was nominated for an Ignatz Award).

Fingerman has broadened his palette, turning to prose, and continuing to work in comics. His most recent offerings are the humor collection ''You Deserved It'', ''Zombie World: Winter's Dregs & Other Stories'', the zombie graphic novel, ''Recess Pieces'', and his debut prose novel, ''Bottomfeeder''.
